competitor_data.pricing: Phyn Plus 2nd Gen MSRP: $499 device. Bundled device + standard installation packages offered through insurance/partner co-branded portals (e.g., $899 all-in for device + up to 4 hrs labor via AAA California program). Plumber installation labor typically ranges $500–$800 separately, set independently by each installer. No mandatory monthly subscription fee — app is free with full core functionality. Pro pricing (discounted wholesale) available to certified Phyn Pro members. Utility/partner rebate programs (e.g., $150 rebate post-installation via Helix WD) reduce effective consumer cost.
threats: The Jonathan Scott acquisition injects celebrity marketing horsepower into Phyn's brand — HGTV's mass audience (predominantly homeowners in the FLO target demographic) will see Phyn prominently featured, crowding FLO's consumer mindshare

Phyn's 15-carrier insurance network (more than double FLO's 7) creates a systematic advantage in the insurance-to-plumber demand pipeline — insurers recommending Phyn to policyholders who then hire a Phyn Pro directly threatens FLO's installer attach rate
ICON Builders joining as investor with explicit mandate to expand Phyn's Preferred Installer Network signals aggressive Pro channel recruitment, potentially poaching FLO's certified plumbers

Phyn's no-subscription positioning ('free app, no monthly fees') is a direct attack on FLO's FloProtect subscription — if plumbers lead with total-cost-of-ownership, Phyn wins on paper even at the same device price

Phyn's ultrasonic sensor technology is genuinely superior in hard-water/high-debris environments — in markets with poor water quality, plumbers may prefer Phyn to reduce warranty claims and callbacks, eroding FLO's installer base

Post-acquisition growth targets (insurance, property management, building, utilities, home services) mirror FLO's own channel priorities — Phyn is now directly competing across every FLO vertical with fresh capital and high-profile backing
Third-party plumber review sites (e.g., callmother.com) now rank Phyn Plus #1 ahead of Flo by Moen, influencing both consumer and professional installer purchase decisions at the top of the funnel
strengths: Patented ultrasonic flow sensor (built exclusively by Badger Meter) with no moving parts — more durable and accurate than turbine-based competitors like Flo by Moen, especially in hard-water environments, a compelling plumber upsell argument

15 insurance carriers (vs. Flo's 7) offer installation rebates or premium discounts for Phyn Plus as of late 2025, giving plumbers a stronger ROI pitch to homeowners and creating strong insurer-driven demand pull
Phyn Pro Program provides plumbers a structured pathway: directory listing, Pro pricing, and marketing support — a lightweight but functional channel program that lowers adoption friction
No mandatory subscription fee is a powerful consumer objection-handler that plumbers can use at point-of-sale, differentiating Phyn from subscription-dependent competitors

High-profile celebrity investor (Jonathan Scott, Property Brothers) provides massive earned media and consumer brand awareness opportunity that directly benefits plumber installers selling to HGTV-watching homeowners
Established multi-year OEM/partner track record (Kohler H2Wise, Uponor Pro Squad, EcoWater Systems) demonstrates ability to embed into existing trade professional networks
Validated by international government program (Western Australia) as preferred leak detection technology — provides third-party credibility that plumbers can reference
2-year warranty (vs. Flo's 1-year) is a tangible plumber and homeowner confidence builder
B2B multi-property dashboard launched for builders and property managers, enabling plumbers to service commercial/multi-unit clients beyond single-family
weaknesses: Phyn Pro Program lacks genuine revenue-sharing or commission structure — plumbers receive directory leads and discounted pricing only, with no recurring service contract revenue stream, making the program less financially sticky than a true reseller model

Phyn explicitly disclaims any agency or employment relationship with Phyn Pros and does not certify, endorse, or monitor installer quality — weakening the program's authority and creating reputational risk
Phyn Plus is explicitly not intended for multi-unit apartment buildings, limiting single-device scalability for plumbers serving MF or commercial clients

Long machine-learning onboarding period (2–3 weeks, requiring ~1,000 water events) generates false alarms during installation period, creating callbacks and negative post-install experiences that reflect on the plumber
Customer support is not 24/7, creating frustration for both homeowners and plumbers during off-hours incidents — a gap that could trigger negative reviews tied to the installer
App is complex and not beginner-friendly compared to Flo by Moen, increasing post-install homeowner support burden on the plumber

Only push notifications (no automated phone calls) for leak alerts — a known complaint vs. Flo's automated calling, reducing perceived urgency and making it harder for plumbers to sell on emergency response value
No alarm system integration limits appeal for homeowners with existing monitored security systems — a gap plumbers must explain away
Hardware is physically large and requires clearance, making tight-space installations difficult and increasing labor time and cost
Revenue model is entirely hardware-centric with no ongoing SaaS or monitoring subscription that could fund plumber service contracts or create recurring revenue
competitor_data.floAdvantage: FLO's key edge is the combination of Moen brand trust, a superior onboarding experience (1-week learning vs. Phyn's 2–3 weeks with fewer false alarms), a more intuitive consumer app, automated phone-call leak alerts, and the FloProtect subscription which — while a cost — enables a recurring-revenue plumber service contract model and $5,000 deductible reimbursement guarantee that Phyn's no-subscription hardware-only model structurally cannot offer.
opportunities: FLO can exploit the absence of a meaningful plumber commission or service contract model — FLO should design a formal recurring-revenue program (e.g., annual monitoring contract split with plumber) that Phyn structurally cannot match with its no-subscription model
FLO's faster 1-week learning phase vs. Phyn's 2–3 weeks is a direct competitive differentiator FLO can arm plumbers with to close deals — 'fewer callbacks, faster activation, happier customers'
FLO's Moen brand backing and superior app UX represent a durable advantage with mainstream (non-power-user) homeowners that plumbers serve most frequently
FLO can build a true plumber reseller/commission program (not just a directory) to attract Phyn Pro installers who are financially underserved by Phyn's model

The Jonathan Scott acquisition creates a celebrity-driven consumer awareness surge for the overall smart water category — FLO should ride category growth while positioning on app simplicity, Moen trust, and FloProtect's ongoing value

Phyn's explicit exclusion of multi-unit apartment buildings creates a white space FLO's MUD Portal and RUBS submetering already address — FLO should equip plumbers with a dual-pitch (SFH + MF) that Phyn cannot offer
FLO can target Phyn Pro installers in markets where insurance carrier coverage is thinner (Phyn has 15 carriers but coverage is not uniform) with tailored carrier partnerships
The Western Australia government program model reveals that large-scale B2B institutional programs (utility, government, builder) are winnable — FLO should prioritize similar program structures
competitor_data.strategicSignal: FLO leadership must act now to counter the Jonathan Scott halo effect by launching a differentiated, financially compelling Phyn Pro poach strategy — specifically a formal plumber commission or recurring-service-contract revenue share program that exploits Phyn's structural inability to offer installer recurring revenue in a no-subscription model — while aggressively expanding its own insurance carrier partnerships to close the 15-vs-7 gap before Phyn's new investors accelerate their insurer channel further.
competitor_data.threatRationale: The May 2025 acquisition by Jonathan Scott's investor group brings celebrity brand-building, ICON Builders' installer network expansion, and fresh capital targeting every FLO vertical simultaneously, while Phyn's superior insurance carrier count (15 vs. 7) and no-subscription positioning directly undercut FLO's two strongest plumber and consumer sales arguments.

Post-acquisition strategy explicitly targets expansion of the Preferred Installer Network and deeper penetration of the Pro/Plumber channel via ICON Builders' sales and operational infrastructure
Partnership with Nationwide Insurance (Dec 2024) offering 15% product discount to high-value home policyholders, driving plumber-installed demand
Partnership with Bamboo Insurance (Aug 2024) offering 10% premium discount and 15% off Phyn products for California homeowners, creating installer pull-through
Partnership with Selective Insurance (Feb 2024) for residential leak detection, further expanding insurer-to-plumber referral pipeline

competitor_data.commercializationModel: Phyn operates a certified installer reseller/referral model branded the 'Phyn Pro Program.' Licensed plumbers apply, are vetted, and gain access to Pro-only wholesale pricing and a directory listing on Phyn's Find-a-Phyn-Pro portal — which also receives traffic from Home Depot, Best Buy, and Costco customers seeking a qualified installer. Phyn earns revenue on hardware sales to or through Pro installers; plumbers earn margin on the device markup plus their independent installation labor fee ($500–$800+). Phyn does not pay commissions or referral fees to plumbers — the value exchange is lead generation via directory listing and access to co-branded insurance/utility partner demand. Insurance carrier partnerships (15 carriers as of late 2025) create downstream consumer pull, funneling insured homeowners toward Phyn-certified plumbers. There is no ongoing SaaS/service contract revenue for plumbers. The model is hardware-centric with a free app and no recurring plumber-side revenue share.

competitor_data.pricing: The L5 WiFi Water Leak Shutoff Valve (Braukmann) is a pro-only SKU — pricing requires a licensed plumber/installer and is not listed publicly on consumer retail channels. Third-party distributor listings (e.g., Jackson Systems, Home Controls) show the L5 valve-only unit in the ~$150–$250 range at trade, with the bundled L5+L1 combo kit likely $200–$350 at distributor cost. The L1 WiFi Water Leak & Freeze Detector retails around $70 (First Alert-branded consumer version). Hardware-only model: no mandatory subscription fee for the homeowner app; Pro-IQ contractor subscription is a separate monthly SaaS charge billed per location to the installing contractor. Total installed job cost (hardware + labor) estimated at $300–$600, significantly below Flo by Moen's ~$500+ hardware-only MSRP before installation.
threats: ADI's 100,000+ contractor relationships at 200+ stocking locations give Resideo structural plumber reach that FLO cannot replicate at comparable scale — Resideo can flood the pro channel with L5 hardware at low friction and marginal cost per installer relationship.

Price undercutting at the installer level: Resideo's estimated trade hardware cost ($150–$250 for the L5 system) is significantly below FLO's ~$500 MSRP, enabling plumbers to offer Resideo as a lower-cost alternative on the same service call where FLO would be the premium option.

Cross-category bundling: Resideo can bundle L-Series water systems with thermostats, security panels, and HVAC diagnostics into a single contractor proposal — making it harder for FLO to win on a standalone water pitch when Resideo offers a whole-home package through one relationship.

Insurance carrier endorsements create market legitimacy: Resideo's Nationwide and USAA partnerships (with published claim reduction data) give Resideo credibility in insurance-adjacent sales conversations that validates the entire pro-installed water shutoff category — potentially raising homeowner awareness of alternatives to FLO.

Resideo's explicit strategic investment in 'Leak Detection / Flow Analytics' as a growth vector: The May 2026 SEC lender presentation signals Resideo intends to upgrade its water sensing capabilities — if Resideo adds flow monitoring or AI analytics to the L5 platform (organically or via acquisition), FLO's core product differentiation narrows significantly.

Pro-IQ contractor subscription creates platform lock-in: Once a plumbing or HVAC firm is subscribed to Pro-IQ (brand in app, remote diagnostics, service call leads), they are economically incentivized to recommend Resideo hardware on every call — creating a switching cost that crowds FLO out of that contractor's recommendation set.

Post-spin P&S entity is a focused residential sensing company: After the ADI separation, the remaining Resideo P&S business will be a pure-play residential controls and sensing company with greater strategic focus and potentially higher R&D allocation to water — making Resideo a sharper, more dangerous competitor post-2026.
strengths: Unmatched pro-channel distribution depth: ADI Global Distribution serves 100,000+ contractors across 200+ stocking locations in 17 countries, giving Resideo near-universal plumber reach that no pure-play water tech competitor can match.

Honeywell Home / First Alert brand halo: 130+ years of residential safety heritage and presence in 150 million homes creates immediate contractor and homeowner trust that significantly lowers the sales cycle compared to newer entrants.

Lower total installed cost for homeowners: The L5 hardware is priced well below Flo by Moen's ~$500 MSRP, making Resideo easier for plumbers to upsell on a service call without sticker-shock objections.

Broad ecosystem cross-sell: Resideo's L-Series is designed as an add-on during HVAC or plumbing service calls (L2 for HVAC condensate, L5 for main line), giving plumbers and HVAC techs a natural upsell motion across multiple visit types.

Pro-IQ contractor retention tool: Monthly subscription service embeds contractor branding in the homeowner's Resideo app, creating a loyalty loop that increases contractor stickiness to the Resideo platform.

Insurance carrier validation: Nationwide study (Feb 2025) showing $4,000 average claim cost reduction, plus USAA PERKS inclusion, gives Resideo credible third-party proof of ROI for contractor sales conversations and insurance-driven demand.
Snap One / Control4 integrator base: Post-acquisition access to Control4 smart-home integrators adds a premium residential channel where water leak systems are a natural bundle component.

Sensor cable flexibility: L1 sensor cable can extend to 500 feet, enabling broad area coverage in basements and utility rooms — a capability advantage over point-sensor-only competitors in retrofit installations.
Resideo Academy training: Mobile-first contractor training app reduces adoption friction for plumbers new to smart water products, accelerating SKU pull-through in the pro channel.
competitor_data.competitor: Resideo Leak Defense (L-Series: L1 Detector + L5 WiFi Shutoff Valve)
weaknesses: No flow monitoring or water analytics: The L5 is a reactive shutoff device triggered by physical contact sensors — it cannot detect slow drips, pressure anomalies, or invisible pipe leaks the way Flo by Moen's FloSense AI does, representing a fundamental product intelligence gap.

No AI/ML micro-leak detection: Unlike Flo's MicroLeak daily pressure tests that can catch leaks as small as one drop per minute, Resideo's system requires water to physically reach a sensor cable or detector — missing the proactive detection use case entirely.

No water usage data or conservation insights: The L5 provides zero water consumption tracking, usage history, or conservation reporting — eliminating Resideo from utility rebate programs, ESG-conscious homeowner segments, and any conservation-linked insurance discount programs.

Hardware-only recurring revenue: Without a homeowner subscription model, Resideo earns revenue only at point of sale — no per-device ARR, making the water business episodic and transaction-driven rather than recurring.

Pro-IQ subscription is contractor-facing only, not homeowner-facing: Resideo cannot build a direct relationship with the homeowner post-install, ceding customer data and future upsell opportunities to the contractor.

No smart home ecosystem integration: The L1 detector does not work with Amazon Alexa or Google Home, limiting appeal to smart-home-forward homeowners and integrators who expect full ecosystem compatibility.

L5 is plumber-exclusive (no DIY path): While this protects the pro channel, it eliminates the large and growing DIY/self-install market segment that Flo by Moen can serve, capping Resideo's total addressable market.

ADI spin-off creates channel uncertainty: The planned H2 2026 separation of ADI Global Distribution into an independent public company introduces organizational disruption, potential relationship gaps, and strategic misalignment risk during transition — a critical window competitors can exploit.

Water is a subscale category within a large conglomerate: Despite stated strategic intent, water leak is a small revenue contributor vs. Resideo's thermostat and security businesses — risk of underfunding R&D and go-to-market investment relative to a focused competitor like FLO.

Buoy Labs water intelligence platform was acquired (2019) and then discontinued (2021): Resideo has already demonstrated it abandoned a more sophisticated water monitoring platform — signaling organizational difficulty in sustaining software-led water innovation.
competitor_data.floAdvantage: FLO's Flo by Moen Smart Water Shutoff delivers AI-powered flow monitoring, MicroLeak daily pressure testing, and continuous water usage analytics that Resideo's L5 system fundamentally cannot replicate — Resideo detects water only when it physically contacts a sensor cable, while FLO detects leaks as small as one drop per minute through inline flow and pressure sensing, no physical water contact required. This intelligence gap, combined with FLO's homeowner-facing FloProtect subscription (direct ARR, data ownership, conservation reporting), creates a defensible moat in both the premium pro-install segment and the insurance/utility program channel where data-driven ROI evidence is required.
opportunities: FLO can own the AI-analytics layer Resideo lacks: By leading with FloSense flow monitoring, MicroLeak detection, and water usage data that Resideo's L5 cannot offer, FLO can position as the intelligent water management platform vs. Resideo's reactive sensor/shutoff.

Target the plumber channel during Resideo's ADI spin-off disruption (H2 2026): The organizational separation of ADI from P&S creates a natural window where plumber relationships may be in flux — FLO should run aggressive installer recruitment and co-op programs during this period.

Capture the DIY-to-Pro upgrade market: Resideo's plumber-only distribution leaves the large self-install and e-commerce segment uncontested — FLO's dual DIY and pro-install positioning can capture both demand pools that Resideo structurally ignores.

Lead on smart home ecosystem integration: Resideo's L1 detector has no Alexa or Google Home compatibility — FLO can differentiate aggressively in the integrator/smart-home channel by offering full ecosystem compatibility that Resideo cannot.

Win insurance carrier programs with data-driven ROI: Resideo's hardware-only model cannot offer carriers the continuous water usage telemetry and behavioral risk scoring that FLO's FloSense platform generates — FLO can build deeper, more defensible insurance partnerships based on live data, not just shutoff events.

FloProtect subscription vs. Resideo's no-homeowner-subscription model: FLO can build direct homeowner ARR and loyalty that Resideo structurally cannot — positioning FloProtect as the premium service tier that creates ongoing homeowner value Resideo is unable to replicate.

Target multifamily and property management where Resideo has no coherent offering: Resideo's L-Series is designed for single-family residential — FLO's MUD portal and per-unit monitoring architecture addresses a segment Resideo is not equipped to serve.

Exploit Resideo's abandoned Buoy Labs narrative: FLO can credibly point to Resideo's discontinuation of Buoy (its only intelligent water monitoring platform) as proof that Resideo lacks commitment to data-driven water intelligence — reinforcing FLO's differentiation in prospect conversations.
competitor_data.strategicSignal: Resideo's planned ADI spin-off (expected H2 2026) is the single most important near-term event FLO leadership must act on: the organizational separation will create a 12–18 month window of contractor relationship flux and channel uncertainty where FLO should aggressively recruit plumber installers, launch co-op programs, and lock in exclusive or preferred-installer agreements before ADI and P&S re-stabilize as independent entities with refreshed channel incentives.
competitor_data.threatRationale: Resideo's unmatched pro-channel distribution (100,000+ contractors, 200+ ADI locations), trusted Honeywell/Braukmann brand heritage, lower hardware price point, and explicitly stated strategic growth investment in leak detection/flow analytics make it the most structurally dangerous pro-channel competitor to FLO's plumber installer business — even though its current product lacks FLO's intelligence capabilities.
recentDevelopments: Resideo announced intention to spin off ADI Global Distribution as an independent public company (announced July 30, 2025; expected to close mid-Q3 to mid-Q4 2026), which will separate the branded hardware P&S business from its massive professional distribution arm — creating potential channel disruption during transition.

Resideo completed its $1.4B acquisition of Snap One (June 2024), integrating Control4 and smart-living integrator tools into ADI, further deepening the pro-installer ecosystem and expanding cross-sell surface area that could include water leak products.

Resideo struck a deal with Honeywell (July 2025) paying a one-time $1.59B to eliminate all future indemnity payment obligations (~$140M/year through 2043), giving the post-separation P&S entity cleaner financials and greater strategic flexibility to invest in water sensing growth.

Resideo's SEC lender presentation (May 2026) explicitly identifies 'Leak Detection / Flow Analytics' as the key sensing-led growth vector within its ~$10B total water addressable market, signaling increased strategic investment and focus in this category.

Nationwide Insurance partnership (published Feb 2025) demonstrated Resideo devices reduced average water claim costs by $4,000 — providing third-party proof of ROI that Resideo now actively uses to drive Pro/Plumber and insurance channel demand.

Resideo expanded its USAA PERKS program relationship (Sept 2023) to all 13 million USAA members, giving insurance-backed demand signals that flow back to plumber-installed premium products like the L5 shutoff valve.

Pro-IQ Services suite — a monthly subscription for contractors — positions Resideo to generate recurring contractor loyalty revenue by embedding the contractor's brand in the Resideo homeowner app, creating a sticky pro ecosystem around water and HVAC products.
Resideo Academy app launched (Feb 2023) offering HVAC, plumbing, and security professionals on-demand mobile training, lowering friction for new plumber adoption of the L-Series water system.
competitor_data.commercializationModel: Resideo monetizes the Pro/Plumber channel primarily through a hardware reseller model: the L5 shutoff valve and L1 detector are manufactured under the Braukmann brand and sold to plumbers, HVAC contractors, and security integrators at trade pricing via ADI Global Distribution (200+ stocking locations globally, 100,000+ contractor relationships) and select North American distributors. The plumber purchases hardware at distributor cost, marks it up, and charges the homeowner for parts + installation labor — Resideo captures margin at the hardware sale, not the installation. A secondary contractor SaaS layer (Pro-IQ Essentials/Advanced, monthly subscription per location) allows contractors to white-label their brand inside the Resideo app, creating a recurring B2B2C revenue stream tied to installed device count. No homeowner-facing subscription is required for basic operation, reducing consumer friction but limiting Resideo's direct recurring revenue per device. Resideo's channel leverage is structural: ADI's 200+ stocking locations mean any plumber with a distributor account can source L-Series hardware same-day, creating near-zero channel friction for adoption at scale.

competitor_data.pricing: SaaS ARPU of $5.67/unit/month (full-year 2025), up ~1% YoY. ARR reached $56.9M as of Q3 2025, up 7% YoY. Revenue model is three-part: (1) upfront hardware sales (Alloy SmartHome hub + devices), (2) professional services for installation/deployment, (3) recurring SaaS subscription fees per deployed unit covering access controls, asset monitoring, and Community WiFi. Hardware pricing is not publicly disclosed; enterprise contracts are negotiated directly. Company is actively moving away from bulk hardware deals toward per-unit SaaS subscriptions as the primary revenue driver.

SmartRent's open Z-Wave platform and hardware-agnostic positioning could allow it to white-label or OEM a water shutoff product (including potentially from competitors) without building it internally, closing the gap quickly

competitor_data.floAdvantage: FLO's Flo by Moen Smart Water Shutoff provides active, automatic whole-unit water shutoff — a capability SmartRent's Alloy Leak Sensor+ entirely lacks. While SmartRent detects and alerts, FLO stops the water. This positions FLO's MUD Property Manager Portal as the only enterprise-grade MDU solution that combines real-time monitoring, automatic shutoff, insurance discount enablement, and water usage data in a single integrated system — delivering provably superior asset protection ROI that SmartRent cannot match with a passive sensor alone.
opportunities: SmartRent's sensor-only water detection gap (no shutoff valve) reveals strong demand for active water shutoff in MDU — FLO's MUD platform with per-unit shutoff directly fills this unserved need within SmartRent's own installed base
SmartRent's financial weakness and leadership instability creates a window for FLO to approach SmartRent's operator customers with superior water protection ROI stories during renewal cycles
SmartRent does not offer RUBS submetering or utility billing — FLO can position its RUBS product as a complementary or replacement solution to operators already using SmartRent for automation
SmartRent's retreat from channel partners and mid-market opens a gap for FLO to build installer and property management relationships with smaller operators (100–2,000 units) underserved by SmartRent

Insurance partnership angle: SmartRent's leak sensors generate alerts but cannot prevent catastrophic water events — FLO's automatic shutoff offers insurance carriers a far stronger risk mitigation argument, enabling FLO to displace SmartRent in insurance-adjacent MDU programs

SmartRent's MDU leak sensor is passive — FLO can position its Property Water Security System (active shutoff + monitoring + water data) as a superior asset protection solution for REITs focused on NOI and ESG

SmartRent's open architecture and integration-first approach means FLO could pursue a platform integration partnership to embed FLO shutoff devices within SmartRent's ecosystem, gaining distribution to 890K+ units

Operator pressure on NOI amid rising costs (labor, insurance, utilities) creates appetite for water cost reduction tools — FLO's water usage data and conservation analytics directly address this gap SmartRent cannot
competitor_data.strategicSignal: SmartRent's financial distress and CEO churn in 2024–2025 create a narrow but time-sensitive window for FLO to aggressively close deals with SmartRent's top-tier operator accounts by leading with active water shutoff ROI (insurance savings, damage prevention, NOI improvement) — capabilities SmartRent cannot match today — but FLO must accelerate MDU sales motions before SmartRent's Vision 2028 stabilization closes this vulnerability.
competitor_data.threatRationale: SmartRent's 890K+ unit installed base, top-20-operator relationships, and embedded leak detection network create a dominant distribution moat that directly competes for FLO's MDU pipeline, even though SmartRent currently lacks active shutoff — a gap that could close at any time given SmartRent's platform investment trajectory.

competitor_data.commercializationModel: SmartRent sells directly to institutional multifamily owners and operators (REITs, large property management firms) via an enterprise B2B model. Operators sign portfolio-level agreements covering hardware deployment + ongoing SaaS subscriptions. The system is rolled out property-by-property through SmartRent's professional services team (not through resellers or plumbers). Revenue flows as: (1) one-time hardware sale per unit, (2) one-time professional services installation fee per unit, (3) monthly SaaS subscription per deployed unit (~$5.67/unit/month). Operators may pass tech costs to residents via bundled rent or ancillary amenity fees. SmartRent's channel partner program was scaled back in 2024. The company's 15-of-top-20-multifamily-operator penetration creates a dominant enterprise land-and-expand motion.

competitor_data.pricing: Custom, portfolio-size-based per-unit pricing with no publicly listed rates. Pricing decreases significantly at scale. Utility Billing, Submetering Services, Invoice Processing, and Energy Analytics are modular add-ons sold on top of a Voyager or Breeze Premier base license. Meter installation and maintenance offered under separate Meter Maintenance or On-Demand service programs. Operators effectively face an all-in bundled cost that requires full commitment to the Yardi ecosystem to unlock maximum value.
threats: Yardi's massive Voyager installed base means any multifamily operator already on Yardi will default to Yardi Utility Billing for submetering and RUBS — effectively locking FLO out of a large portion of the addressable market without a Yardi integration partnership.

Yardi's convergent billing (utility + rent on one statement) is a proven retention mechanism that makes it extremely difficult for FLO to displace Yardi as the primary resident billing touchpoint once Yardi is embedded.

Yardi is actively expanding its leak detection product line (rope sensors, smart alerts, 24/7 monitoring) — if it adds automated shutoff or partners with a shutoff hardware vendor, FLO's key differentiator in the multifamily segment narrows significantly.

Yardi's 24/7 live support infrastructure and dedicated regulatory compliance team set a high service-level bar that FLO's smaller organization must match to compete for enterprise and institutional multifamily accounts.

strengths: Deep platform lock-in: All utility billing calculations, RUBS allocations, and submetering data flow natively inside Yardi Voyager, eliminating any need for external interfaces and making switching costly for large operators.

End-to-end stack ownership: Yardi controls meter hardware installation, ongoing maintenance, leak detection, billing, invoice processing, and analytics — a true single-vendor solution for multifamily utility management.

Convergent billing drives NOI recovery: Combining utility charges with rent on a single ledger statement demonstrably raises collection rates and has been shown to increase utility cost recovery by up to 20%.

Compliance infrastructure: Yardi maintains a dedicated regulatory staff that conducts jurisdiction-specific legal reviews for every enrolled property, reducing operator exposure to class-action risk from billing errors.
Proven enterprise scale: Clients such as Simon Konover Company (80+ properties) recovered $370,000 in water/sewer charges in a single year using Yardi's end-to-end submetering and billing suite.
24/7 live support via YES Energy Management: Operators have access to a dedicated specialist call center, a meaningful differentiator for large portfolio managers.
ENERGY STAR benchmarking and ESG reporting built into the platform, addressing growing institutional investor and regulatory sustainability disclosure demands.

weaknesses: Ecosystem lock-in is a barrier to entry — non-Yardi customers cannot access Yardi Utility Billing, making it irrelevant to the large share of multifamily operators on RealPage, Entrata, AppFolio, or MRI.

Steep learning curve and complex implementation: User reviews consistently cite that setup costs can equal or exceed purchase cost, and that training demands are heavy — disadvantaging mid-market and smaller operators.

No real-time automated shutoff capability: Yardi Leak Detection uses rope sensors and smart alerts but does not offer flow-based intelligent water shutoff at the unit or building level — a critical gap vs. FLO's hardware.
Leak detection is passive and alert-based, not proactive: Yardi flags abnormal readings but cannot autonomously stop a leak event, meaning water damage still occurs between alert and human response.
Pricing is opaque and custom: No transparent per-unit or per-property rates are publicly available, creating friction in mid-market sales cycles where operators want fast ROI calculations.

Antitrust reputational overhang: The ongoing Duffy v. Yardi federal class action (not dismissed as of April 2026), combined with the FPI co-defendant settlement and cooperation agreement, keeps Yardi under legal and media scrutiny — a concern for compliance-focused institutional operators.

Hardware dependency on third-party submeter vendors (NextCentury, Metron): Yardi is an integrator, not a hardware manufacturer, meaning submeter quality and data latency are controlled by partners, not Yardi.

Utility management value proposition is billing/recovery-centric, not loss-prevention-centric — operators focused on infrastructure protection and insurance premium reduction find the solution inadequate.
Too complex and costly for independent landlords and small portfolios, ceding the sub-500-unit market segment to simpler competitors.

opportunities: FLO can target the large multifamily operator segment that runs on non-Yardi platforms (RealPage, Entrata, AppFolio, MRI) with a hardware-first, software-agnostic water intelligence solution that integrates via API — a segment Yardi cannot serve.

competitor_data.pricing: Three-tier revenue model: (1) Hardware/Device Solutions — per-unit capital sale of smart meters, communication modules (e.g., Gen5 500W water modules, Intelis wSource ultrasonic meters), and leak sensors sold via multi-year utility contracts; (2) Networked Solutions — project-based deployment of IIoT AMI/AMR networks billed as capex programs with long multi-year delivery cycles; (3) Outcomes/SaaS — per-endpoint, per-month subscription pricing for analytics platforms (Itron Analytics, UtilityIQ, Temetra MDM), with a base toolkit fee plus modular add-on pricing per analytics application. SaaS contracts include software, cloud infrastructure, and maintenance for the subscription duration. Contract values span from mid-five-figures for small utilities to multi-million-dollar enterprise deals for large municipal utilities (e.g., LADWP, GRU Gainesville). ARR of $368M as of year-end 2025.
threats: Itron's utility relationships and brand trust give it a direct channel to pitch expanded in-home or customer-premises leak solutions — if Itron acquires a consumer-side shutoff company or builds an in-home extension, it could instantly lock FLO out of utility distribution programs.

strengths: Dominant AMI market position with 8,000+ utility customers in 100+ countries and over 285M communicating endpoints delivered — making Itron the de facto standard infrastructure layer for water, electric, and gas utilities globally.

Deep regulatory and procurement moat: utilities buy through formal RFP processes with long qualification timelines, and Itron's certified, integrated infrastructure creates prohibitive switching costs once deployed.

weaknesses: Itron's leak detection stops at the utility distribution system boundary (street meter) — it has no in-home or behind-the-meter shutoff capability, meaning it cannot detect or stop leaks inside the customer's property, which is FLO's core domain.

Massive enterprise sales cycles and RFP procurement timelines (often 12–36 months) make Itron structurally unable to serve small and mid-size water utilities or move at the speed of individual homeowner or property manager demand.

Hardware-heavy cost structure: full AMI deployments require large utility CAPEX commitments, making Itron inaccessible to smaller utilities, rural co-ops, or municipalities without access to federal infrastructure funding.
Itron's solution is utility-operator-centric — end consumers and homeowners have no direct interface, no individual shutoff control, and no insurance or property protection value proposition.

Complexity and integration burden: Itron projects require CIS/OMS/MDM integration, certified installer networks, and long deployment timelines — creating friction that smaller or faster-moving market entrants can exploit.

Revenue declined 3% in full-year 2025 ($2.4B vs prior year) with near-term macroeconomic uncertainty and trade policy volatility cited as headwinds, suggesting some cyclical vulnerability in utility capex spend.

No direct consumer-facing shutoff valve product — Itron's leak sensing is acoustic/flow-based at the distribution network level, not an in-line whole-home shutoff, which is precisely what FLO by Moen delivers.
competitor_data.floAdvantage: FLO operates entirely on the customer-premises side of the meter — detecting, alerting, and physically shutting off water inside the home or building — a domain Itron structurally cannot serve with its distribution-network AMI platform. FLO's whole-home flow monitoring and auto-shutoff capability addresses the leak damage liability gap that Itron explicitly cannot close (leaks between street meter and dwelling), making FLO the natural complement — or competitive wedge — in any utility water loss program.
opportunities: FLO can position as the 'last mile' complement to Itron's AMI infrastructure — where Itron monitors the distribution network to the meter, FLO monitors and controls water from the meter into the home, filling Itron's structural blind spot.

Utility rebate and conservation program integration: Itron's utility partners already run consumer engagement programs; FLO can partner with the same utilities to offer subsidized smart shutoff valves as a rebate program hardware benefit, piggybacking on Itron's customer relationships.

Itron's NRW programs prove that utilities are willing to invest in leak reduction ROI — FLO can bring the same ROI narrative to the property side of the meter where Itron cannot go, targeting utilities that want to extend leak reduction to customer premises.

Small and mid-size utility segment: Itron's minimum viable contract scale excludes many smaller water utilities — FLO can target these underserved utilities with a lower-cost, faster-to-deploy smart shutoff program, potentially as a utility-subsidized consumer hardware bundle.

Insurance carrier channel: Itron has no insurance product or carrier relationship — FLO can lock up the insurance channel as a distribution moat before Itron or its partners attempt to extend into home protection.

Multifamily/property manager angle: Itron's Temetra MDM manages utility-side data; FLO's MUD Portal addresses the property-side water management gap (per-unit submetering, RUBS, leak shutoff) that Itron's utility tools don't serve.

API/data partnership opportunity: FLO could explore data-sharing integrations with Itron's Temetra or UtilityIQ platforms to provide utilities with property-side leak data as a complementary analytics layer, deepening FLO's value in utility conservation programs.

Federal infrastructure funding tailwinds (IIJA water provisions) are increasing utility AMI procurement — FLO can ride the same wave by positioning smart shutoff valves as eligible premises-side infrastructure eligible for utility conservation program subsidies.
competitor_data.strategicSignal: Itron's 20% ARR growth, $368M recurring revenue base, and dual acquisitions of Urbint and Locusview signal a deliberate push to own the full utility asset lifecycle — FLO leadership must act now to establish utility rebate program partnerships and data integration agreements with Itron's utility customers before Itron closes the 'last mile' gap by acquiring or bundling a consumer-premises smart shutoff solution into its next-generation AMI platform.
competitor_data.threatRationale: Itron is a dominant utility infrastructure platform but operates at the distribution network level and has no in-home shutoff or consumer-premises product, creating a structural blind spot that FLO occupies — however, Itron's scale, utility relationships, and aggressive acquisition strategy pose a credible future encroachment risk if Itron moves to bundle premises-side solutions into its AMI RFP packages.

Acquired Locusview (utility-focused digital construction management SaaS, $525M) in January 2026 to extend lifecycle services from planning through maintenance.

Ended 2025 with $368M in annual recurring revenue, growing 20% year-over-year, and $4.5B total backlog as of mid-2025.

Deployed Intelis wSource NB-IoT ultrasonic smart water meters across major international utilities including Watercare Services (Auckland, NZ — 100,000 meters), Hunter Water (NSW, Australia — 2,000-meter pilot Q3 2026), and Publiacqua (Tuscany, Italy).
Expanded smart water deployments to Fiji Islands (Water Authority of Fiji), Corsica, and Thessaloniki, Greece in 2025–2026.
Signed Perth Smart Water Meter Pilot with Water Corporation (WA) using Temetra cloud MDM platform for 16,000 smart meters.

competitor_data.commercializationModel: Itron sells directly to water utilities (municipal, investor-owned, and cooperative) via formal RFP-driven procurement cycles. Revenue flows through three segments: (1) Device Solutions — one-time hardware capex recognized on delivery; (2) Networked Solutions — project-based revenue recognized over deployment milestones, often multi-year; (3) Outcomes — recurring SaaS and managed services fees billed annually on a per-endpoint, per-month basis. Utilities are locked into Itron's ecosystem through long product qualification cycles, deep CIS/OMS/MDM integration, switching costs, and cybersecurity certification requirements. Itron also leverages a partner/reseller ecosystem of 250+ partners (e.g., Border States, United Systems, Pacific Technologies) who distribute and install Itron hardware regionally. Reseller agreements with SaaS providers (e.g., Smart Energy Water) extend Itron's reach into utility customer engagement. Contracts are typically 5–15 year infrastructure programs with follow-on software and maintenance annuities, creating durable revenue streams tied to the meter refresh cycle.

competitor_data.pricing: Hardware (ORION endpoints + E-Series ultrasonic meters) sold upfront via bulk municipal procurement contracts. BEACON SaaS billed monthly per endpoint ('Service Units') — publicly documented at ~$0.88/meter/month for AMI reads in one municipal contract, escalating $0.01/year over a 15-year term. NaaS (Network-as-a-Service) bundles cellular backhaul infrastructure, gateway supply/maintenance, and BEACON hosting into a single per-endpoint monthly subscription, eliminating separate infrastructure capex for utilities. EyeOnWater consumer engagement portal included in NaaS tier. SaaS/data analytics billed per endpoint with mid-to-high gross margins; volume discounts and tiered pricing increase ARPU at scale. Bundled bids (meter + endpoint + BEACON SaaS) drive procurement wins.
threats: Utility gateway risk: If Badger Meter begins offering or OEM-partnering on in-home/property-level smart shutoff devices (through future acquisition or partnership), it could disintermediate FLO from utility distribution channels FLO depends on.

Perception substitution: Utility procurement officers who adopt Badger's full BlueEdge platform may believe they have 'solved' their water intelligence problem, reducing urgency to add FLO's residential layer — even though the two address different points in the water network.

Data monopoly at network level: Badger Meter's BEACON platform controls utility-level consumption and leak data; if utilities choose to share only aggregated AMI data, FLO's value proposition of granular in-home data becomes harder to differentiate or integrate.

Municipal influence over ratepayer hardware choices: Utilities locked into Badger's ecosystem (sole-source contracts) may steer ratepayers toward Badger-compatible consumer tools, limiting FLO's ability to penetrate utility-sponsored rebate programs in those markets.

Competitive legitimacy halo: Badger Meter's scale (3,000+ utilities, landmark PRASA contract, NYSE-listed, $220M+ quarterly revenue) creates a high-trust institutional credibility that makes it difficult for FLO to compete on brand authority in utility-facing conversations.

Expanding wastewater and stormwater monitoring via SmartCover signals Badger Meter's intent to own the entire water cycle infrastructure budget — potentially crowding out third-party integrations and ecosystem partnerships FLO might rely on.
strengths: Dominant market position: ORION endpoints deployed at 3,000+ U.S. water utilities with 10M+ endpoints in the field, representing enormous installed base and switching costs.

Cellular NaaS differentiation: Pioneered AMI without dedicated fixed-network infrastructure, using existing cellular networks — eliminating utility need for tower permits, leased infrastructure, or capex-heavy deployments.

Full-stack vertical integration: BlueEdge suite covers metering hardware, ORION connectivity, BEACON SaaS analytics, EyeOnWater consumer portal, and now SmartCover sewer/stormwater monitoring — a single-source end-to-end platform.

Proprietary lock-in: BEACON SaaS and ORION are exclusive to Badger Meter, enabling sole-source procurement designations (e.g., Chicago DWM) and structurally high switching costs across long-term agreements.
Landmark reference contract: PRASA win (1.6M meters, largest single-utility AMI project globally) adds massive credibility for future municipal contract bids.
Strong financial profile: 39–42% normalized gross margin, high-single-digit 5-year revenue CAGR, and robust SaaS growth signal a durable, scalable business.
ESG and sustainability narrative: 27 billion gallons of water savings attributed to Cellular AMI installed base strengthens utility buyer and regulatory appeal.

Expanding into wastewater/collection monitoring via SmartCover (~50% U.S. market share in sewer monitoring), a segment described as less than 1% digitally penetrated in North America — providing a large greenfield TAM.
weaknesses: Utility-only B2B focus: Badger Meter has no residential consumer product, no in-home shutoff, and no direct homeowner relationship — making it irrelevant to insurance carrier programs or consumer water damage prevention at the property level.

Hardware dependency: Despite SaaS growth, utility water solutions still drive ~70–75% of revenue; exposure to municipal budget cycles, procurement delays, and tariff/commodity cost volatility remains high.

Long sales cycles and lumpy revenue: Municipal contracts take months-to-years to close and deploy, creating revenue unpredictability — evidenced by Q4 2025 miss and H1 2026 slowdown guidance despite strong pipeline.

No property-level shutoff or active leak intervention: Badger Meter detects and alerts on leaks at the utility network level but does not provide property-level automated water shutoff — a critical gap for insurance-integrated or residential use cases.

Proprietary ecosystem creates vendor concentration risk for utilities: Long-term sole-source contracts may face procurement scrutiny or regulatory pushback as municipalities modernize procurement practices.
Stock price decline (~30% over 1 year as of mid-2026) suggests investor concern about near-term growth deceleration and execution risk on large-scale deployments like PRASA.
Limited international presence: North America accounts for ~80%+ of sales; global expansion is nascent despite PRASA being a U.S. territory project.
SmartCover integration risk: $185M acquisition at ~5x revenue is a significant premium; integration execution risk could distract from core AMI growth.
competitor_data.floAdvantage: FLO operates at the property/premise layer — providing active automated shutoff, pressure monitoring, and real-time household leak detection — a layer Badger Meter's utility-network AMI platform fundamentally cannot reach; FLO is the downstream action layer to Badger's upstream sensing infrastructure, making the two potentially complementary but with FLO uniquely positioned to own the homeowner, insurer, and property manager relationships that Badger Meter structurally cannot address.
opportunities: FLO can partner with utilities as a downstream residential complement to Badger's AMI — where Badger detects leaks at the distribution network, FLO detects and shuts off at the property line, creating a utility-branded leak prevention bundle.

Utility conservation program integration: FLO's device + FloProtect plan could be offered by utilities as a ratepayer incentive program (rebate or co-pay model), addressing the Non-Revenue Water problem at the household level where Badger Meter stops.

FLO can target the residential/multifamily layer that Badger's B2B-only model ignores — capturing the growing homeowner and property manager demand for active leak prevention that utilities cannot fulfill with AMI alone.

Insurance carrier distribution: Badger Meter has no insurance channel presence; FLO can dominate carrier-funded hardware bundles, deductible programs, and rate discount integrations that are structurally inaccessible to a utility meter company.

The PRASA deployment and global AMI upgrade cycle signal massive utility modernization spending — FLO can approach utilities in AMI upgrade mode to co-launch ratepayer engagement programs pairing Badger's network visibility with FLO's in-home action layer.

Smart water meters market growing at 11.9% CAGR to $9.04B by 2030, driven by NRW reduction mandates — FLO can position as the consumer-facing NRW reduction solution that AMI infrastructure alone cannot deliver.
FLO's MUD/RUBS submetering and multifamily offerings address a vertical Badger Meter does not serve with a dedicated product, creating a clear competitive moat in the property management channel.
competitor_data.strategicSignal: Badger Meter's record-setting PRASA contract (1.6M meters) and SmartCover acquisition signal it is racing to own the entire utility water cycle from distribution network to collection system — FLO leadership should immediately develop a utility partnership narrative positioning FLO's in-home shutoff as the 'last mile' complement to Badger's AMI infrastructure, and approach utilities in active AMI upgrade cycles (especially FEMA-funded municipal modernization projects) to embed FLO devices into ratepayer conservation and leak prevention programs before Badger or a competitor fills that residential gap.

competitor_data.commercializationModel: Badger Meter operates a hardware-anchored, SaaS-recurring revenue model exclusively for water utilities and municipalities. Revenue flows through three layers: (1) Upfront hardware sales — ORION cellular/fixed endpoints and E-Series ultrasonic meters sold via multi-year AMI rollout contracts and bulk municipal procurement, often sole-sourced; (2) Monthly per-endpoint SaaS subscriptions — BEACON software, hosting, analytics, and OTA updates billed as 'Service Units' directly to the utility or via authorized distributors over multi-year (often 15-year) agreements; (3) NaaS managed service — Badger bundles cellular network backhaul, gateway deployment/maintenance, and BEACON SaaS into a single recurring per-endpoint fee, removing the need for utility-owned fixed network infrastructure. This creates deep lock-in: BEACON and ORION are proprietary and cannot be sourced from any other vendor. Cross-sell of SmartCover sewer monitoring and BlueEdge analytics extends ACV per utility relationship. Utility revenue represents ~70–75% of total company revenue; SaaS/data is the fastest-growing segment at mid-to-high teen CAGR.

competitor_data.pricing: Carrier-distributed hardware bundled free or heavily subsidized to policyholders; end-consumer pays recurring monitoring fees: Security360 (personal lines) at $15.99/month on a 36-month agreement via USAA; Water Security360 (WS360) at $5.99/month on a 12-month agreement; early termination fees of $400/Y1, $200/Y2, $100/Y3 for Security360 and $70/Y1 for WS360. Retail MSRP for standalone water/freeze sensor is $49.99. Carrier-facing pricing is bespoke/enterprise (not publicly disclosed). Commercial Protection360 pricing is quote-based for carrier programs.

competitor_data.commercializationModel: Pure B2B2C insurance channel model: Roost sells turn-key, white-labeled telematics programs directly to P&C insurance carriers, who then deploy hardware and branded apps to their policyholders. Revenue flows from (1) hardware unit economics subsidized or sold through carriers at volume, (2) monthly recurring SaaS/monitoring fees split between Roost and carrier per program agreement, and (3) data analytics and actuarial insight services sold back to carriers to inform underwriting and loss ratio management. Carriers absorb upfront hardware cost in exchange for claims savings, NPS lift, retention, and underwriting data. Roost handles all fulfillment, customer journey automation, alert notification engine, 24/7 professional monitoring dispatch integration, and white-labeled mobile app — operating as a full-stack turnkey vendor. Strategic investors include carrier-side backers (Aviva Ventures, Desjardins Insurance, USAA Ventures), blurring the line between customer and investor.

competitor_data.pricing: Carrier-funded free-to-policyholder model: carriers pay Ting Labs per-sensor program fees (hardware + multi-year monitoring service); policyholders receive sensor, app, and 3–5 years of fire prevention service at no cost, plus a $1,000 hazard remediation credit. Direct retail price is $99 (sensor + first year service) and $49/year thereafter. Carriers never bill policyholders — Ting Labs is invoiced at the program/enterprise level by the carrier or through HSB as distribution intermediary.

Nationwide expanded strategic partnership in March 2026 to deploy 500,000 additional Ting sensors over two years, building on 80,000 homes already protected and 1,100+ fires prevented
Selective Insurance added Ting to its value-added services lineup in January 2025, further broadening carrier base
Triple-I and Whisker Labs published first-ever quantitative IoT claims reduction study (July 2025): Ting reduced non-cat fire claims by 63% within 3 years, saving $81/customer/year
Ting expanded into frozen pipe / water loss prevention in December 2025, combining in-home temperature sensing with grid intelligence to detect frozen pipe risk before damage occurs
Adaptive Insurance integrated Ting Insights into GridProtect parametric power outage insurance product (July 2025), extending Ting's data monetization into B2B commercial lines
vipHomeLink partnered with Whisker Labs (September 2024) to distribute Ting to additional insurance carriers via homeowner engagement platform; install base approaching 900,000+ at time of deal
Farm Bureau Insurance of Michigan launched Ting program in September 2024
Ting Insights grid intelligence platform now covers 94% of U.S. homes within one mile of a sensor, enabling AI-powered real-time grid fault detection
